def_en: lying down; resting in a horizontal, relaxed position
def_zh: recumbent limbs, recumbent posture, recumbent figure
quote_en: "On one side of the river the golden foothill slopes curve up to the strong and rocky Gabilan mountains, but on the valley side the water is lined with trees - willows fresh and green with every spring, carrying in their lower leaf junctures the debris of the winter's flooding; and sycamores with mottled, white, recumbent limbs and branches that arch over the pool." – The narrator describes the landscape by the Salinas River (Chapter 1)
quote_zh: Steinbeck's choice of 'recumbent' for the sycamore limbs invests the natural setting with an almost human repose. The adjective connotes a restful, horizontal posture, suggesting the trees are reclining like weary travellers. This personification foreshadows the site's function as both a refuge and a place of ultimate endings—the pool where Lennie will hide and die. The 'recumbent' limbs, worn smooth by men who have sat on them, link the wilderness to human weariness. The word sets a tone of deceptive calm: nature appears to offer rest, yet the novel will prove that no one on the margins can truly lie down in peace.
ex_en: The recumbent sycamore limb, polished by generations of homeless men, offered Lennie a temporary bed that was both sanctuary and trap.
ex_zh: The tree's recumbent branches draped over the pool like the arms of a tired mother too exhausted to hold her children any longer.

def_en: marked with spots or patches of different colours; not uniform
def_zh: mottled bark, mottled skin, mottled light
quote_en: "On one side of the river the golden foothill slopes curve up to the strong and rocky Gabilan mountains, but on the valley side the water is lined with trees - willows fresh and green with every spring, carrying in their lower leaf junctures the debris of the winter's flooding; and sycamores with mottled, white, recumbent limbs and branches that arch over the pool." – The narrator describing the trees (Chapter 1)
quote_zh: The word 'mottled' captures the variegated, patchy whiteness of the sycamore bark, a detail that could be merely descriptive. However, Steinbeck uses this irregular pattern to mirror the broken, incomplete lives of the men who gather there. The mottled surface is like a visual foreshadowing of Lennie’s fragmented mind and the patchwork nature of the dream—never quite coherent, always marred by darker spots. It suggests that even the beauty of the natural world is not flawless, just as the 'fatta the lan'' is an ideal that will never be fully realised.
ex_en: The mottled sycamores, with their uneven white patches, resembled the imperfect and vulnerable bond between George and Lennie.
ex_zh: Sunlight filtered through the mottled leaves, dappling the ground with a memory of wholeness that had long since splintered.

def_en: to camp or sleep rough, especially outdoors without shelter
def_zh: jungle‑up for the night, jungle‑up by the river, jungle‑up near water
quote_en: "There is a path through the willows and among the sycamores, a path beaten hard by boys coming down from the ranches to swim in the deep pool, and beaten hard by tramps who come wearily down from the highway in the evening to jungle‑up near water." – The narrator describing the path and its users (Chapter 1)
quote_zh: The colloquial verb 'jungle‑up' transforms the act of camping into something primal and survivalist. It evokes a jungle—a wild, untamed space—and suggests that the men who stop here are more like animals seeking a watering hole than civilised people. Steinbeck uses this improvised term to underscore the rootlessness of itinerant workers; they have no home, only temporary dens. The phrase also links to Lennie's later retreat to the brush and the novel’s broader comparison of human beings to beasts, a pattern that culminates in the tragic equation of Lennie with a coyote.
ex_en: To jungle‑up by the green pool was the closest thing to a home that many bindle stiffs would ever know.
ex_zh: The exhausted men jungled‑up under the stars, their bindles poking out of the shadows like tortoises retracting into shells.

def_en: moving with stiff, elevated legs; like walking on stilts
def_zh: stilted gait, stilted heron, stilted motion
quote_en: "The rabbits hurried noiselessly for cover. A stilted heron labored up into the air and pounded down river." – The narrator describing wildlife after the men’s arrival (Chapter 1)
quote_zh: By calling the heron 'stilted', Steinbeck draws attention to its long, awkward legs, but the word also carries a sense of artificiality and strain. The heron appears almost mechanical, laboring into flight, which mirrors the heavy, clumsy efforts of Lennie, who is often compared to a bear. The bird’s departure at the men’s approach symbolises the shattering of natural peace by human presence. The stilted movement hints at the precariousness of existence in this landscape: even the native creatures must struggle to rise, foreshadowing the uphill battle the characters will face in trying to escape their circumstances.
ex_en: The stilted heron, lifting itself with effort from the pool, resembled the immigrant workers who stood on the fragile stilts of their dreams.
ex_zh: The heron rose with a stilted grace, as if the air were thick with the weight of all the secrets the river had swallowed.

def_en: a large fish‑eating wading bird with long legs, an S‑shaped neck, and a long pointed bill
def_zh: heron wading, heron in flight, heron's nest
quote_en: "The rabbits hurried noiselessly for cover. A stilted heron labored up into the air and pounded down river." – The narrator (Chapter 1)
quote_zh: The heron is more than a realistic detail; it is a recurring image of predation and silence, later to reappear at the pool in Chapter 6 to swallow a water snake just before George kills Lennie. Here, its 'stilted' flight is a mimetic echo of the men's own awkward journey. Steinbeck establishes nature as a silent observer, its creatures mirroring human actions. The heron’s sudden departure at the arrival of George and Lennie creates a sense that their presence disturbs an ancient rhythm. The bird’s eventual return at the moment of death implicates nature in the novel’s fatalistic universe, where killing is simply another part of the cycle.
ex_en: The heron’s patient stillness at the water’s edge, waiting for prey, mirrored the way fate waited for the ranch hands.
ex_zh: Time passed in heartbeats and heron flaps, each wingbeat a page turned in the story of the river.

def_en: in a sullen, gloomy, or ill‑tempered manner
def_zh: stare morosely, morosely silent, morosely contemplating
quote_en: "George stared morosely at the water. The rims of his eyes were red with sun glare." – George after complaining about the bus driver (Chapter 1)
quote_zh: When George stares 'morosely' at the pool, the adverb layers his physical exhaustion with a deeper emotional weariness. It is not simply sadness; it is a brooding, irritable gloom that suggests his frustration with Lennie, the heat, and the futility of their wandering. This mood foreshadows the novel’s end, where George will stare at the same water with a far heavier grief. The word imbues the opening scene with a prophetic melancholy, signalling that this idyllic spot is already stained by hopelessness.
ex_en: George's morose gaze into the green water revealed a man who had long stopped expecting anything but trouble.
ex_zh: The evening settled over him like a morose blanket, its threads woven from exhausted sunlight and the dust of miles.

def_en: a small bundle of belongings rolled in a blanket and carried over the shoulder; a bedroll
def_zh: sling a bindle, carry a bindle, unpack a bindle
quote_en: "George unslung his bindle and dropped it gently on the bank." – The narrator describing George’s actions (Chapter 1)
quote_zh: The bindle is the quintessential emblem of the itinerant worker’s life—a portable, meagre home. The gentleness with which George handles his bindle, in contrast to his harsh words, reveals a hidden tenderness for the little he owns. The bindle contains their beans, work cards, and the fragile structure of their shared existence. It is both a burden and a life‑support system, much like Lennie himself. As the novel progresses, the bindle vanishes, replaced by the dream of a fixed home, but the bindle’s symbolism remains: all that a man has can be rolled in a blanket, and all can be lost.
ex_en: Each man’s bindle held his entire past and future, yet weighed lighter than loneliness.
ex_zh: The bindle lay against the sycamore root like a tired heart, the blanket’s folds holding the shape of miles walked.

def_en: a line of people or things following one after another
def_zh: walk in single file, proceed single file, line in single file
quote_en: "They had walked in single file down the path, and even in the open one stayed behind the other." – The narrator (Chapter 1)
quote_zh: The image of the two men walking 'single file' immediately establishes the hierarchy and dependency of their relationship. George leads; Lennie follows. Steinbeck uses this spatial arrangement to visually encode the power dynamic: George is the guide, Lennie the follower. Even in open space, they do not walk side by side, suggesting that their bond, while deep, is not an equal partnership. This motif continues until the final chapter, where Lennie walks to his death alone, and George must follow to end the file.
ex_en: They moved through the world in single file, George clearing a path that Lennie’s shadow would never fill.
ex_zh: The years stretched out behind them like a single file of footprints, each one a story told in the dust.

def_en: to speak in a monotonous, low tone; to murmur repeatedly
def_zh: droned on, droned softly, droned to himself
quote_en: "Lennie droned to himself softly, 'I ain't gonna say nothin'.... I ain't gonna say nothin'.... I ain't gonna say nothin'.'" – Lennie reciting George’s orders (Chapter 1)
quote_zh: The verb 'droned' captures Lennie’s repetitive, mechanical recitation as he tries to remember George’s command. The word suggests an insect‑like hum or a mindless chant, underscoring Lennie’s cognitive limitations. Yet this droning is also a form of self‑soothing, a prayer‑like ritual that calms his fears. Steinbeck uses the auditory image of the drone to illustrate how Lennie internalises George’s voice as a form of conscience. The drone is both pathetic and protective, a verbal tether between the two men.
ex_en: Lennie’s droning repetition of George’s rules revealed a desperate attempt to cage his own unpredictable strength.
ex_zh: The promise droned in his mind like a fly trapped against glass, buzzing hope that could never find a way out.

def_en: in a blunt, abrupt, and often unfriendly manner
def_zh: said brusquely, brusquely dismissed, brusquely ordered
quote_en: "George sat up. 'Awright,' he said brusquely. 'Gi'me that mouse!'" – George to Lennie after finding the mouse (Chapter 1)
quote_zh: George’s brusque tone when demanding the mouse is a defence against the tenderness he feels for Lennie. The adverb reveals an abruptness that borders on cruelty, but throughout the novel Steinbeck makes clear that George’s brusqueness is a performance, a way to cope with the burden of care. This verbal sharpness contrasts with the softness of his actions, like the way he later washes his hands and reasons with Lennie. The word encapsulates the paradox of their relationship: love expressed through harshness, kindness through commands.
ex_en: George spoke brusquely to mask the affection that, if acknowledged, would make his life unbearable.
ex_zh: The night wind answered the tent flaps with a brusque slap, as if telling the campers to stop dreaming and start moving.

def_en: imitating someone’s speech or mannerisms, often to mock
def_zh: mimicking someone, mimicking voice, mimicking gesture
quote_en: "He took on the elaborate manner of little girls when they are mimicking one another. 'Jus' wanted to feel that girl's dress - jus' wanted to pet it like it was a mouse - Well, how the hell did she know you jus' wanted to feel her dress?'" – George cruelly imitating Lennie’s explanation for what happened in Weed (Chapter 1)
quote_zh: George’s 'mimicking' of Lennie is a bitter, theatrical performance that vents his accumulated frustration. By adopting the elaborate, mocking tone of little girls teasing one another, he reduces Lennie's disastrous innocence to a childish tantrum—yet the mimicry is so exact that it betrays how deeply George understands Lennie’s mind. He vocalises the very words Lennie cannot articulate, the simple, terrifying logic of wanting to touch something soft. The passage turns George into both accuser and translator, and the cruelty of his mimicry is inseparable from the intimacy it reveals. Steinbeck uses the word to expose the paradox at the heart of their bond: only through mockery can George fully express the truth of Lennie’s experience, a truth that will ultimately destroy them both.
ex_en: George’s mimicking of Lennie was a weapon of frustration that also proved he knew his companion’s soul better than anyone else ever would.
ex_zh: The mockingbird outside the bunkhouse window was mimicking the sound of a key turning in a lock, as if nature itself practiced the art of escape.

def_en: showing severe mental or physical pain; tormented
def_zh: anguished cry, anguished expression, anguished soul
quote_en: "He looked across the fire at Lennie's anguished face, and then he looked ashamedly at the flames." – George’s reaction after yelling at Lennie (Chapter 1)
quote_zh: Lennie’s 'anguished face' is the catalyst for George’s shame. The adjective ‘anguished’ conveys profound suffering, yet it is a suffering caused by words, not physical blows. Steinbeck implies that Lennie, despite his mental simplicity, feels emotional pain deeply, and it is this visible anguish that prevents George from abandoning him. The moment turns the campfire into a confessional, the flames reflecting the guilt that burns in George. This anguish prefigures the ultimate mercy George must show, which will be both the greatest kindness and the deepest cause of anguish.
ex_en: Lennie’s anguished expression after George’s tirade revealed that even the simplest soul can be shattered by words.
ex_zh: The anguished howl of a distant coyote threaded through the canyon, stitching the night sky to the earth with threads of sorrow.

def_en: a device with mirrors used to see above the surface, but here a simile for the snake’s raised head
def_zh: periscope head, like a periscope, periscope vision
quote_en: "A water snake slipped along on the pool, its head held up like a little periscope. The reeds jerked slightly in the current." – The narrator describing the wildlife (Chapter 1)
quote_zh: The simile comparing the water snake’s head to a 'periscope' introduces a mechanical, almost military image into the natural scene. It suggests surveillance and hidden danger, transforming the snake into a spy. This image recurs in the final chapter when a similar snake is eaten by the heron, creating a symbolic loop of watching and being watched. The periscope metaphor also reflects George’s constant vigilance, scanning the environment for threats to Lennie, and hints that the natural world is not innocent but perpetually on the lookout.
ex_en: The water snake’s periscope head scanned the surface as if nature were spying on the men who had invaded its sanctuary.
ex_zh: The truth surfaced in the conversation like a periscope, a tiny eye of honesty that quickly submerged again into the murky water of lies.

def_en: involving many carefully arranged details; intricate and fanciful
def_zh: elaborate manner, elaborate plan, elaborate design
quote_en: "He took on the elaborate manner of little girls when they are mimicking one another." – The narrator describing George’s performance before he launches into his cruel imitation (Chapter 1)
quote_zh: George’s ‘elaborate’ manner when mocking Lennie suggests a performance that is overdone, almost theatrical. The adjective highlights the gap between the crude reality of the men’s lives and the sophistication of George’s mimicry. It shows that George is capable of complex, symbolic thought, a sharp contrast to Lennie. Yet this elaboration is used to belittle; it is a weapon forged from his own intelligence. Thus, the word illuminates a tragic irony: the same mental capacity that makes George a caretaker also enables him to be cruel, a cruelty he must constantly atone for.
ex_en: George’s elaborate imitation was a shield of words, protecting him from the simple truth that he loved the man he mocked.
ex_zh: The sunset painted an elaborate farewell on the horizon, a tapestry of gold and crimson that mocked the simple greyness of the bunkhouse.

def_en: small furry mammals with long ears, but in the novel a symbol of the shared dream of independence
def_zh: tend the rabbits, talk about rabbits, dream of rabbits
quote_en: "'I remember about the rabbits, George.' 'The hell with the rabbits. That's all you ever can remember is them rabbits.'" – Lennie and George in a tense exchange after Lennie forgets their destination (Chapter 1)
quote_zh: Throughout the novel, 'rabbits' function as a recurring motif for the dream of independence and Lennie’s childlike innocence. In this early exchange, Lennie’s fixation on the rabbits reveals his need for something soft to care for, even as he cannot recall their immediate plans. George’s bitter retort—‘The hell with the rabbits’—shows his exasperation, yet the rabbits remain the single detail Lennie reliably remembers, a symbol of the one future he can hold onto. The word is repeated like a litany, becoming a secular prayer for a future that will never materialise. The tragic irony is that Lennie’s very desire to tend rabbits leads him to kill Curley’s wife, destroying the dream.
ex_en: The rabbits shimmered in Lennie’s mind as the ultimate symbol of a future where his strength could be tender.
ex_zh: Hope multiplied in their minds like rabbits, breeding faster than they could fence it in.

def_en: an amount of money saved or earned, especially for a specific purpose
def_zh: build a stake, blow a stake, roll up a stake
quote_en: "They come to a ranch an' work up a stake and then they go into town and blow their stake, and the first thing you know they're poundin' their tail on some other ranch." – George describing typical ranch hands (Chapter 1)
quote_zh: The word 'stake' transforms money into a transient, unstable entity. It connotes gambling—a 'stake' is what one bets—and Steinbeck uses it to show that the men’s earnings are never invested but gambled on ephemeral pleasures. The phrase 'blow their stake' suggests violent dissipation, like air escaping, mirroring the emptiness of their lives. For George and Lennie, the 'stake' they try to accumulate becomes the cornerstone of their dream; yet the gambling metaphor persists, for their plan is the biggest gamble of all, and they will lose.
ex_en: Every dollar toiled for was part of a stake that promised a future, only to be spent on momentary escapes from the present.
ex_zh: Their hope was a fragile stake driven into the hard soil of reality, trembling at the first wind of misfortune.

def_en: a dense growth of bushes and small trees
def_zh: hide in the brush, dash into the brush, under the brush
quote_en: "Look, Lennie – if you jus' happen to get in trouble like you always done before, I want you to come right here an' hide in the brush." – George instructing Lennie (Chapter 1)
quote_zh: The 'brush' serves as a geographical and symbolic refuge throughout the novel. It is the place of hiding, of secrecy, and ultimately, of death. By instructing Lennie to hide in the brush, George establishes a ritual of retreat that mirrors Lennie’s regression to a childlike state when frightened. The brush becomes both a sanctuary and a trap; it conceals but also confines. At the end, the brush witnesses the most intimate act of mercy. The word resonates with the novel’s themes of escape and the impossibility of true concealment from one’s nature.
ex_en: The brush by the river was Lennie’s only geography of safety, a green embrace that would eventually tighten into a noose.
ex_zh: Memories of the ranch clung to him like burrs from the brush, small and sharp, impossible to pick off entirely.

def_en: a wild canine of North America, often regarded as vermin; here used to dehumanise Lennie
def_zh: shoot a coyote, for a coyote, like a coyote
quote_en: "Somebody'd shoot you for a coyote if you was by yourself." – George to Lennie during their argument (Chapter 1)
quote_zh: George’s comparison of Lennie to a coyote is brutally practical: without George, Lennie would be mistaken for a dangerous animal and shot. The statement equates Lennie’s lack of social understanding with animal status, a recurring theme. However, the coyote is also a creature of the wild, adaptable and resilient, yet despised. By invoking this image, George acknowledges society’s view of Lennie as vermin, and his own role as the only barrier between Lennie and a bullet. The word carries the weight of the novel’s tragic climax, where George himself must become the shooter to save Lennie from a worse fate.
ex_en: Lennie, in George’s eyes, was a gentle giant but to the world he was a coyote to be destroyed.
ex_zh: Loneliness roamed the bunkhouse at night like a coyote, its howls echoing in the hollow spaces between men’s breaths.

def_en: a handyman or cleaner who does odd jobs, especially mopping and sweeping
def_zh: old swamper, ranch swamper, work as a swamper
quote_en: "The old swamper looked quickly at him, and then shuffled to the door rubbing his whiskers with his knuckles as he went. 'Them guys just come,' he said, and shuffled past the boss and out the door." – Candy responding to the boss's arrival and deferentially leaving the bunkhouse (Chapter 2)
quote_zh: Steinbeck labels Candy as 'the swamper' rather than by name, embedding his identity in his menial role. The noun derives from 'swamp', meaning to clean or mop out, and immediately marks Candy as the lowest rung of the ranch’s labour hierarchy. His shuffling exit—rubbing his whiskers, deferring to the boss—amplifies this lowliness. The swamper is the one who cleans up after others, a living premonition of what happens to a man who grows old and disabled on the ranch. Candy’s title thus functions as a warning: this is the future that awaits every bindle stiff who stays too long, making the dream of a little place all the more desperate.
ex_en: Candy, the swamper, mopped floors that would eventually swallow him up, his usefulness measured in brooms and dustpans.
ex_zh: The swamper's broom whispered across the bunkhouse floor like a secret no one bothered to hear.

def_en: slang for lice or parasitic insects that infest bedding and clothing
def_zh: riddled with graybacks, get graybacks, clean of graybacks
quote_en: "'Tell you what —' he said finally, 'last guy that had this bed was a blacksmith — hell of a nice fella and clean a guy as you want to meet. Used to wash his hands even after he ate.' 'Then how come he got graybacks?' George working up a slow anger." – The old swamper defending the previous bunk occupant while George demands proof of cleanliness (Chapter 2)
quote_zh: The colloquial term ‘graybacks’ reduces the menace of lice to something almost mundane, yet its casual deployment underscores the squalor of the bunkhouse. George’s indignation is not just about hygiene; it is about dignity. The fear of infestation symbolises the creeping degradation of the itinerant life—no matter how clean a man may be, the environment always threatens to contaminate him. The word also links to the novel’s broader animal imagery, as lice are parasites that feed on the living, much like the ranch feeds on the bodies of its workers until they are used up and cast aside.
ex_en: The threat of graybacks in the bunkhouse beds foreshadowed the inescapable rot that clung to every hired hand.
ex_zh: Poverty burrowed into their lives like graybacks, tiny and invisible until the itching became unbearable.

def_en: in a manner showing doubt or disbelief; not easily convinced
def_zh: said skeptically, look skeptically, ask skeptically
quote_en: "'I ain't so sure,' said George skeptically. 'What did you say he quit for?'" – George questioning the old man's praise of the blacksmith's cleanliness (Chapter 2)
quote_zh: George’s skeptical response reveals his hard-won wariness. Having been misled by bus drivers and burnt by employers, he no longer trusts flattering stories. The adverb signals a survival instinct; skepticism is his intellectual armour against a world that offers little kindness without a catch. Steinbeck uses the word to contrast George’s cautious, analytical mind with Lennie’s guileless openness. George’s doubt, however, is not cynicism but a pragmatic defence, one that will ultimately be justified when the dream is shattered.
ex_en: George’s skeptical nature, forged by years of disappointment, made him the perfect guardian for a man who believed everything he was told.
ex_zh: Hope knocked on the door of his heart, but skepticism, that old watchdog, growled from the corner.

def_en: soothed; pacified; reduced in anger or severity
def_zh: slightly mollified, mollified tone, mollified by apology
quote_en: "'Damn right he don't,' said George, slightly mollified, 'not if he wants to stay workin' long.'" – George after the old swamper's assurance that ranch hands don't pry (Chapter 2)
quote_zh: When George is ‘slightly mollified’, the phrase captures the precariousness of his temper and the ease with which minor reassurances can defuse his anger. ‘Mollify’ derives from Latin ‘mollis’, meaning soft; George’s hard edges are momentarily softened by the swamper’s deferential respect. This emotional modulation recurs throughout the novel, as George oscillates between fury and tenderness toward Lennie. The partial nature—‘slightly’—acknowledges that no comfort is complete in a world where suspicion is the currency of survival.
ex_en: George, mollified by the swamper's defence, let his guard down just enough to accept a temporary refuge.
ex_zh: The heat of his rage cooled into a mollified mist, the steam of anger condensing into the quiet acceptance of another day.

def_en: a black man who works in the stables; a derogatory ranch term for an African-American stable hand
def_zh: the stable buck, call the stable buck, stable buck's room
quote_en: "The old man said, 'I guess the boss'll be out here in a minute. He was sure burned when you wasn't here this morning. Come right in when we was eatin' breakfast and says, "Where the hell's them new men?" An' he give the stable buck hell, too.' George patted a wrinkle out of his bed, and sat down. 'Give the stable buck hell?' he asked. 'Sure. Ya see the stable buck's a nigger.'" – Candy matter-of-factly explaining the hierarchy to George (Chapter 2)
quote_zh: The term ‘stable buck’ is a compound that fuses occupation with racial designation, reducing a man to his job and his skin colour in one stroke. Candy delivers the information without self-consciousness, revealing how deeply naturalised the racism of the ranch is. The word ‘buck’ is particularly dehumanising, linking Crooks to animal stock. Yet Crooks subverts this reduction; he reads, he possesses a shotgun, and he guards his private space fiercely. Steinbeck uses the label to show how a man can be imprisoned by a job title that refuses him full humanity, making Crooks’s isolation a political condition rather than a personal choice.
ex_en: The stable buck’s title stripped him of a name and confined him to a barn, as if his soul could be stabled like a horse.
ex_zh: The words 'stable buck' hung around Crooks’s neck like a yoke, heavy and polished by years of bitter use.

def_en: a profoundly offensive racial slur for a black person; in the novel, it reflects the casual racism of 1930s America
def_zh: call a nigger, treated like a nigger, nigger work
quote_en: "From the distance came a clear call. 'Stable Buck—ooh, sta-able Buck!' And then, 'Where the hell is that God damn nigger?'" – An anonymous voice from the grain teams calling for Crooks (Chapter 2)
quote_zh: This slur is embedded in the novel as a deliberate reflection of the historical setting, not as endorsement but as indictment. The voice calling ‘Where the hell is that God damn nigger?’ is faceless, anonymous—a chorus of the ranch’s ingrained prejudice. Steinbeck uses the word to expose how racism is woven into the everyday language of working men, so normalised that it requires no speaker identification. The slur isolates Crooks utterly, making him a target even from voices offstage. Its brutality prepares the reader for Chapter 4, where Crooks will articulate the damage such words have inflicted on his soul.
ex_en: The word 'nigger' was the lock on Crooks’s door, more solid than wood, keeping everyone out and him in.
ex_zh: The slur landed on the stable floor like a horseshoe, heavy and ringing, bending the silence into a shape of shame.

def_en: the lead driver of a team of mules, who controls all the animals with a single long rein (the jerkline)
def_zh: expert jerkline skinner, head jerkline skinner, praise the jerkline skinner
quote_en: "He was a jerkline skinner, the prince of the ranch, capable of driving ten, sixteen, even twenty mules with a single line to the leaders. He was capable of killing a fly on the wheeler's butt with a bull whip without touching the mule. There was a gravity in his manner and a quiet so profound that all talk stopped when he spoke. His authority was so great that his word was taken on any subject, be it politics or love. This was Slim, the jerkline skinner." – The narrator introducing Slim with his full mythic stature (Chapter 2)
quote_zh: By introducing Slim as ‘the jerkline skinner’ and then layering on this remarkable catalogue of skills, Steinbeck grants him a regal status before a single line of dialogue. The term is occupational royalty; a jerkline skinner could drive up to twenty mules with one line, a feat requiring an almost mystical rapport with animals. The noun functions as a mark of competence and authority, explaining why Slim’s word is law on the ranch. This title, earned by skill rather than birth, contrasts with Curley’s inherited status as boss’s son. In the moral economy of the novel, the jerkline skinner is the true prince, and his quiet judgments carry the weight of justice.
ex_en: Slim, the jerkline skinner, commanded mules and men alike with the gentle authority of someone who understood that force was less powerful than understanding.
ex_zh: The title 'jerkline skinner' sat on Slim’s shoulders like a well-worn coat, the leather of it softened by years of steady hands on a single line.

def_en: eager or quick to fight; aggressive and quarrelsome
def_zh: pugnacious glance, pugnacious attitude, pugnacious little man
quote_en: "He glanced coldly at George and then at Lennie. His arms gradually bent at the elbows and his hands closed into fists. He stiffened and went into a slight crouch. His glance was at once calculating and pugnacious." – The narrator describing Curley’s first assessment of the new men (Chapter 2)
quote_zh: Steinbeck pairs ‘pugnacious’ with ‘calculating’ to reveal Curley as a man who fights with strategy, not just anger. The adjective, from Latin ‘pugnare’ (to fight), labels Curley as a boxer who treats every social encounter as a potential bout. His pugnacity is a performance of masculinity, yet it is also a symptom of insecurity; the little man must constantly prove himself against bigger opponents. The word foreshadows the violence Curley will instigate, violence that will cascade into the tragedy because Curley’s pugnacious nature cannot tolerate being laughed at or ignored.
ex_en: Curley’s pugnacious stare sized up every man as a potential opponent in a ring that existed only in his mind.
ex_zh: Pugnacity coiled in his small fists like a spring, always wound tight and waiting for the slightest tremor to release it.

def_en: thinking carefully and shrewdly, often for personal advantage
def_zh: calculating glance, calculating mind, calculating move
quote_en: "He glanced coldly at George and then at Lennie. His arms gradually bent at the elbows and his hands closed into fists. He stiffened and went into a slight crouch. His glance was at once calculating and pugnacious." – The narrator describing Curley’s stare (Chapter 2)
quote_zh: The adjective ‘calculating’ betrays Curley’s cold, strategic assessment of George and Lennie. He is not merely aggressive but is measuring his chances, weighing reach and size like a fighter in the ring. Steinbeck suggests that Curley’s aggression is never mindless; it is a deliberate tool of domination. This calculation makes him more dangerous than a purely impulsive bully, for he chooses his victims carefully. The word also exposes the tragic flaw in Curley’s logic: he cannot calculate for Lennie’s unpredictable strength, which operates outside the rules of boxing and reason alike.
ex_en: Curley’s calculating eyes passed over the newcomers, cataloguing threats the way a rancher appraises livestock.
ex_zh: Her affection was calculating, distributed like paycheques, each smile accounted for in a ledger of debts.

def_en: in a way that suggests something bad will happen in the future
def_zh: said ominously, ominously quiet, hover ominously
quote_en: "He said ominously, 'Well, he better watch out for Lennie. Lennie ain't no fighter, but Lennie's strong and quick, and Lennie don't know no rules.'" – George warning Candy about the potential conflict between Curley and Lennie (Chapter 2)
quote_zh: George’s ominous tone transforms a simple warning into a prophecy. The adverb signals that George senses a collision course between Curley’s aggression and Lennie’s brute strength. It is a moment of tragic foresight: the reader is alerted that Lennie, who acts without malice or comprehension of rules, will be his own worst enemy. The ominousness is not about weather or external fate but about character, and Steinbeck uses George’s prescience to heighten the dramatic irony—the audience now waits for the inevitable violence that George has predicted but cannot prevent.
ex_en: The ominous silence in the bunkhouse after Curley’s exit foretold a storm that no one could outrun.
ex_zh: The clouds massed ominously above the ranch like fists refusing to unclench, their knuckles bruised with unshed rain.

def_en: to draw oneself up angrily; to show resentment or offended pride
def_zh: bridled at the remark, bridled a little, bridled with indignation
quote_en: "Lennie's eyes moved down over her body, and though she did not seem to be looking at Lennie she bridled a little. She looked at her fingernails. 'Sometimes Curley's in here,' she explained." – The narrator describing Curley’s wife's reaction to Lennie’s stare (Chapter 2)
quote_zh: The verb ‘bridled’ draws on equestrian imagery, likening Curley’s wife to a horse that tenses under a rein. She pulls herself up in response to Lennie’s gaze, a gesture of offended modesty that is undercut by her heavily made‑up appearance. The word captures the paradox of her character: she courts attention yet bristles when it arrives from the wrong source. The bridle is also an instrument of control, and the verb implies that she is perpetually straining against the constraints of her marriage, her clothes, and her isolation, a tension that will snap in the barn.
ex_en: Curley’s wife bridled at Lennie’s stare, a flash of pride that did not match the desperation of her situation.
ex_zh: Her vanity bridled like a startled mare every time a compliment refused to come.

def_en: coloured with red cosmetic; heavily and artificially red
def_zh: rouged lips, rouged cheeks, rouged face
quote_en: "She had full, rouged lips and wide-spaced eyes, heavily made up." – The narrator describing Curley's wife (Chapter 2)
quote_zh: The word ‘rouged’ immediately signals artificiality, performance, and a desire to be seen. By emphasising the heaviness of the makeup, Steinbeck presents Curley’s wife not as naturally pretty but as constructed, almost painted on. The rouge is a mask, a signal of her yearning to be somewhere glamorous, yet it also marks her as a tart in the eyes of the ranch men. The adjective aligns with the novel’s suspicion of surfaces that promise more than they deliver; her red lips will utter dreams of Hollywood that, like rouge, smear and fade in the harsh light.
ex_en: Her rouged lips were the brightest colour in the bunkhouse, a red flag of longing in a world of grey denim.
ex_zh: The sunset rouged the horizon’s cheeks, a cosmetic flush that could not hide the bruise of night waiting beneath.

def_en: backless slippers, often with feathers, associated with glamour and domestic leisure
def_zh: red mules, feathered mules, wear mules
quote_en: "She wore a cotton house dress and red mules, on the insteps of which were little bouquets of red ostrich feathers." – The narrator describing Curley's wife's footwear (Chapter 2)
quote_zh: The 'red mules' are a brilliant detail that both defines and diminishes Curley’s wife. Mules are backless slippers, impractical for ranch life, signalling that she is out of place. The bouquets of ostrich feathers are frivolous, decorative, and faintly absurd against the dust and manure of the barnyard. Steinbeck uses the footwear to communicate her forced domesticity and her attempts to cling to a femininity that the ranch men can only interpret as sexual availability. The mules are her costume of a life she never had, emblems of a glamour that mocks her.
ex_en: The red mules with their feathered bouquets were the footwear of a woman who dressed for a world that existed only in her dreams.
ex_zh: Each step in her red mules scattered tiny feathers on the barn floor, like promises that lost their shape the moment they landed.

def_en: a derogatory slang term for a woman considered sexually promiscuous or of loose morals
def_zh: married a tart, call her a tart, look like a tart
quote_en: "'Well, you look her over, mister. You see if she ain't a tart.' He stepped out the door into the brilliant sunshine." – Candy confiding his opinion of Curley's wife to George as he leaves the bunkhouse (Chapter 2)
quote_zh: Candy’s use of ‘tart’ reduces Curley’s wife to a moral cliché, but Steinbeck complicates the judgment. While the ranch men label her a tart, the novel reveals her as desperately lonely, trapped in a loveless marriage, and grasping for any human connection. The word tells us as much about the men who use it as it does about her; it is a defence mechanism, a way to distance themselves from temptation by blaming the woman. Steinbeck deploys the slur to build the misogyny that isolates her and, tragically, drives her into the barn with Lennie.
ex_en: The label ‘tart’ was stitched onto Curley’s wife by men who feared desire more than they understood loneliness.
ex_zh: Gossip baked her reputation like a tart left too long in the oven, the crust of truth burnt away and only the bitter filling remaining.

def_en: anxious or fearful that something bad will happen
def_zh: suddenly apprehensive, apprehensive glance, nervously apprehensive
quote_en: "She was suddenly apprehensive. 'Bye, boys,' she called into the bunk house, and she hurried away." – The narrator after Slim mentions seeing Curley heading home (Chapter 2)
quote_zh: Curley’s wife’s sudden apprehension reveals the fear beneath her flirtatious facade. The adjective signals that her boldness is performance; underneath, she is terrified of Curley’s anger. This flicker of anxiety offers the first hint that she, too, is trapped in a relationship defined by violence and surveillance. Steinbeck uses the word to invite a fleeting sympathy, a glimpse at the frightened girl behind the rouged mask. Her apprehension is the rational response of someone living in constant threat, making her later fate not a punishment but a torment.
ex_en: The apprehensive flash in her eyes, quickly masked, betrayed that her husband’s temper was a daily terror.
ex_zh: Apprehension seeped into the room like the chill from an ill-fitting door, invisible but impossible to ignore.

def_en: showing a critical or disrespectful attitude; belittling
def_zh: derogatory statement, derogatory remark, derogatory term
quote_en: "The old man was reassured. He had drawn a derogatory statement from George. He felt safe now, and he spoke more confidently." – The narrator after George insults Curley's vaseline glove (Chapter 2)
quote_zh: The phrase ‘derogatory statement’ elevates George’s sarcastic remark into something socially significant. By using this formal adjective, Steinbeck frames the conversation as a ritual of male bonding through shared contempt. The derogatory remark about Curley’s glove full of vaseline becomes a currency of trust between George and Candy; they trade insults to build alliance. The moment reveals how community on the ranch is constructed through collective disdain, a temporary solidarity that will evaporate when real danger arrives.
ex_en: George’s derogatory comment about Curley’s glove was a ticket into the fellowship of men who despised their boss’s son.
ex_zh: Derogatory whispers circled the bunkhouse like flies around a wound, feeding on the weakness of the absent.

def_en: in a deliberately cruel or violent manner
def_zh: said viciously, turned viciously, laughed viciously
quote_en: "'I wasn't kicked in the head with no horse, was I, George?' 'Be a damn good thing if you was,' George said viciously. 'Save ever'body a hell of a lot of trouble.'" – George responding to Lennie's question about whether he was kicked in the head (Chapter 2)
quote_zh: George’s vicious reply—that it would be better if Lennie had been kicked in the head—is the verbal equivalent of a punch. The adverb exposes the cruelty George is capable of, yet it is a cruelty born not of malice but of exhaustion. Steinbeck uses this harshness to show that George’s caretaking is not saintly; it exacts a toll, and the bitterness must escape somewhere. The viciousness is, paradoxically, a sign of intimacy; only someone closely bound could wound so efficiently. George will spend the rest of the novel atoning for this moment.
ex_en: The words came out more viciously than George intended, the weariness of a lifetime sharpening his tongue.
ex_zh: The mare kicked back viciously at the stable door, splinters flying like the fragments of a patience that had long since shattered.

def_en: good at fighting; skilled with one's fists
def_zh: pretty handy, handy fighter, prove handy
quote_en: "'Curley's pretty handy. He done quite a bit in the ring. He's a lightweight, and he's handy.'" – Candy warning George about Curley (Chapter 2)
quote_zh: Candy’s description of Curley as ‘handy’ domesticates violence into a skill, like being good with a hammer. The adjective normalises the constant threat of physical confrontation on the ranch, where a man’s worth is partly measured by his ability to inflict damage. However, Steinbeck undercuts Curley’s handiness by revealing it as a limited currency: it only works within the rules of boxing. Against Lennie, who knows no rules, handiness is useless. The word thus becomes an ironic comment on the fragility of masculine posturing.
ex_en: Curley’s reputation as handy with his fists made him a terror to some, but to Lennie he was merely a puzzle with no solution.
ex_zh: Trouble had a handy way of finding the bunkhouse, slipping through the door like a regular who never paid his tab.

def_en: sharp metal attachments on a rider's boot heels, used to urge a horse forward; also a symbol of status as a non‑labourer
def_zh: wear spurs, spurs jingling, spurs as symbol
quote_en: "On his head was a soiled brown Stetson hat, and he wore high-heeled boots and spurs to prove he was not a laboring man." – The narrator describing the boss (Chapter 2)
quote_zh: The spurs the boss wears are purely decorative, a costume of authority rather than a functional tool. Steinbeck explicitly states they are worn ‘to prove he was not a laboring man’, making the spurs a symbol of class division. They mark the boss as someone who sits on horseback while others walk behind mules. The detail exposes the performance of status on the ranch; every man signals his place in the hierarchy through clothing. The spurs jingle with a power that is real but also theatrical, a sound of privilege that Lennie and George will never make.
ex_en: The jingle of the boss’s spurs was a sound of freedom that the bindle stiffs, walking miles on foot, would never produce.
ex_zh: The boss’s spurs sang a metallic little song of authority, a tune that stopped every conversation it harmonised with.

def_en: slang for mouth; the part of the body that talks
def_zh: big flapper, shut your flapper, keep your flapper shut
quote_en: "When the sound of his footsteps had died away George turned on Lennie. 'So you wasn't gonna say a word. You was gonna leave your big flapper shut and leave me do the talkin'. Damn near lost us the job.'" – George scolding Lennie for almost speaking to the boss (Chapter 2)
quote_zh: George’s use of ‘flapper’ reduces Lennie’s mouth to a mechanical part, an appendage that flaps without thought. The colloquialism is affectionate in its crudeness; it is the language of long familiarity, half insult, half endearment. By framing Lennie’s speech as a liability that must be kept shut, Steinbeck underscores the power imbalance: George owns the voice in this partnership. The word also carries a shadow of the novel’s larger tragedy, for when Lennie’s flapper does open at the wrong moment—to cry out, to speak to Curley’s wife—it sets the gears of catastrophe in motion.
ex_en: Lennie’s flapper was a dangerous instrument, capable of unleashing words that could sink their plans like a stone.
ex_zh: Silence clamped over the bunkhouse like a giant flapper shutting, the conversation swallowed whole.

def_en: dialect form of 'wrestle'; to grapple or struggle physically
def_zh: rassel grain bags, rassel with, rassel someone down
quote_en: "'He can rassel grain bags, drive cultivator. He can do anything. Just give him a try.'" – George praising Lennie to the boss (Chapter 2)
quote_zh: The dialect verb ‘rassel’ does double work: it signals the rural, uneducated register of the men while also capturing the physical, unrefined nature of Lennie’s labour. To ‘rassel’ grain bags is to wrestle them, as if they were opponents. Steinbeck uses the word to convey that Lennie’s strength is his entire identity in the eyes of an employer; he is valued only for what his body can do. The word also foreshadows the physical grappling that will end Curley’s wife’s life—when Lennie rassles with a living thing, the outcome is always fatal.
ex_en: Lennie could rassel a four-hundred-pound bale, but he could not wrestle the simple rules of human interaction into submission.
ex_zh: The river rassled with the rocks all night, a relentless argument that neither current nor stone would ever win.

def_en: a trap designed to catch rats; metaphorically, a dangerous situation or person that ensnares the unwary
def_zh: walk into a rattrap, like a rattrap, set a rattrap
quote_en: "'Well, you keep away from her, 'cause she's a rattrap if I ever seen one. You let Curley take the rap. He let himself in for it. Glove fulla vaseline,' George said disgustedly." – George warning Lennie about Curley's wife (Chapter 2)
quote_zh: George’s metaphor of the ‘rattrap’ crystallises the danger Curley’s wife represents, but it also reveals the novel’s tragic determinism. A rattrap does not pursue its victim; it sits, baited and waiting, and the victim walks in. By framing her as the trap rather than the bait, George acknowledges that the danger lies as much in Lennie’s own nature—his compulsion to touch soft things—as in her provocation. The image reduces her agency to that of a sprung mechanism, a dehumanisation that mirrors the way the ranch has already objectified her. The tragic irony is that George’s warning, however accurate, cannot prevent Lennie from stepping into the trap.
ex_en: Curley’s wife was a rattrap baited with loneliness, its jaws waiting for anyone desperate enough for company.
ex_zh: The promise of easy money snapped shut like a rattrap, and all that remained was the echo of the spring.

def_en: lost in thought; preoccupied and mildly dazed, often by something wonderful or puzzling
def_zh: bemused by the beauty, bemused silence, sit bemused
quote_en: "They all sat still, all bemused by the beauty of the thing, each mind was popped into the future when this lovely thing should come about." – The narrator after Candy joins the dream of the farm (Chapter 3)
quote_zh: Steinbeck's choice of 'bemused' captures the trance-like state the three men fall into when the dream of the little farm suddenly seems possible. The adjective connotes a gentle bewilderment, a happy stupor that suspends their usual defences. It is not simple daydreaming; it is the shock of hope arriving unbidden. The word suggests that the dream, however beautiful, is also a kind of intoxication that separates them from reality. This bemused silence prefigures the crash to come, for the dream is a spell that must break. Steinbeck uses the word to show how the dispossessed cling to visions so fiercely that they become momentarily lost in them, a fugitive ecstasy that makes the coming loss all the more cruel.
ex_en: The three men sat bemused, their shared vision of a little farm lifting them momentarily above the grime of the bunkhouse.
ex_zh: Hope drifted through the room like smoke, and they inhaled it bemused, not knowing it would sting their eyes long after the fire died.

def_en: to cry out in a weak, high-pitched, trembling voice, like a sheep or goat
def_zh: bleated with terror, bleated for help, bleated pitifully
quote_en: "Lennie covered his face with his huge paws and bleated with terror. He cried, 'Make 'um stop, George.'" – Lennie while Curley is beating him (Chapter 3)
quote_zh: The verb 'bleated' reduces Lennie to an animal in the moment of his greatest vulnerability. Steinbeck could have used 'cried' or 'shouted', but 'bleated' explicitly aligns Lennie with a helpless lamb that cannot defend itself. The word evokes the sound of a creature that is prey, not predator, despite Lennie's immense physical strength. This animalisation is sympathetic rather than mocking; it underscores Lennie's innocence and his inability to comprehend violence directed at him. The bleat is the sound of a gentle giant reduced to a terrified child, and it heightens the injustice of Curley's attack, making the reader share George's protective fury.
ex_en: Lennie bleated like a frightened animal, his gigantic strength meaningless against the terror that paralysed him.
ex_zh: The bleated plea hung in the air like the last note of a sheep separated from its flock, unanswered and swallowed by the dark.

def_en: crouching down in fear; shrinking and trembling from something threatening
def_zh: cowering against the wall, cowering in fear, cowering like a dog
quote_en: "Suddenly Lennie let go his hold. He crouch cowering against the wall. 'You tole me to, George,' he said miserably." – Lennie after crushing Curley's hand (Chapter 3)
quote_zh: Lennie's 'cowering' posture turns the aggressor back into a victim. Although he has just broken every bone in Curley's hand, Lennie crouches as if he expects punishment. The present participle 'cowering' emphasises an ongoing state of fear, not a single flinch. Steinbeck uses this image to illustrate Lennie's complete disconnection from his own power; he cannot comprehend that he has won, only that he has done something that might anger George. The cowering figure against the wall is a portrait of a man who has never felt safe, whose instinct is always to shrink from consequences he cannot predict.
ex_en: Lennie cowered after the fight, his enormous frame folding into itself as if trying to disappear from the violence he could not understand.
ex_zh: Fear cowered in the corner of the bunkhouse like a beaten dog, flinching at every raised voice.

def_en: contemptuous ridicule or mockery; scornful laughter
def_zh: voices of derision, mockery and derision, howls of derision
quote_en: "Through the open door came the thuds and occasional clangs of a horseshoe game, and now and then the sound of voices raised in approval or derision." – The narrator setting the scene (Chapter 3)
quote_zh: The word 'derision' introduces the social texture of the ranch: men bond through competitive mockery as much as through approval. It is a world where ridicule is a currency of belonging, where to be laughed at is to be accepted, yet it also hints at a latent cruelty. The horseshoe players' derisive shouts prefigure the way the men will later turn on one another—Curley's humiliation, the laughter that might greet Lennie's mistakes. Steinbeck places the word early in the chapter as an auditory backdrop, a reminder that communal life on the ranch is always edged with the potential for scorn, a foreshadowing of the mockery that Curley will fear if his injured hand is exposed.
ex_en: The constant murmur of derision from the horseshoe pit was the soundtrack of a world where a man's status rose as others fell.
ex_zh: Derision rolled through the bunkhouse door like a stray horseshoe, its iron ring knocking against every man's hidden insecurities.

def_en: a painful medical condition involving stiffness and inflammation of the joints and muscles
def_zh: stiff with rheumatism, suffer from rheumatism, rheumatism in the joints
quote_en: "'He's all stiff with rheumatism. He ain't no good to you, Candy. An' he ain't no good to himself.'" – Carlson arguing for the dog's death (Chapter 3)
quote_zh: 'Rheumatism' is the clinical word Carlson deploys to make his case for euthanasia sound rational rather than cruel. By naming the dog's suffering in medical terms, Carlson transforms sentimental attachment into a problem to be solved. The word's cold, diagnostic quality contrasts with Candy's inarticulate love, and Steinbeck uses this contrast to expose the ranch's utilitarian logic: anything that cannot work, whether a dog or a man, is 'no good'. The rheumatism that stiffens the old dog's body is the physical manifestation of time's toll, and it foreshadows Candy's own admission that he will soon be put 'on the county'. The body that fails is the body that must be disposed of.
ex_en: The rheumatism that crippled Candy's dog was a slow rehearsal for the fate that awaited every man who outlived his usefulness.
ex_zh: Rheumatism crept into her memories, stiffening them until they could no longer bend to accommodate the present.

def_en: deserving strong criticism or condemnation; shameful
def_zh: something reprehensible, caught doing something reprehensible, morally reprehensible
quote_en: "When Candy spoke they both jumped as though they had been caught doing something reprehensible." – The narrator after George and Lennie flinch at Candy's voice (Chapter 3)
quote_zh: The word 'reprehensible' is striking because nothing shameful has occurred; three men are merely discussing a dream. Yet George and Lennie react as if caught in a crime, revealing how deeply the dream itself feels illicit. The adjective exposes the internalised shame of the dispossessed, who have been conditioned to believe that wanting more than wages is a kind of transgression. Steinbeck uses this reflexive guilt to show that the men have so internalised their place at the bottom that even hope feels like a forbidden act, something to hide from the boss and the other hands. The dream is, in the world's eyes, a reprehensible ambition—an overstepping.
ex_en: The dream of a farm felt so dangerously fragile that even discussing it seemed a reprehensible act of defiance against fate.
ex_zh: The secret lay between them like something reprehensible, a stolen jewel that glowed brighter because it was hidden.

def_en: to bring under control; to quiet or restrain
def_zh: subdued one hand, subdued by force, subdued the impulse
quote_en: "Slim gazed at him for a moment and then looked down at his hands; he subdued one hand with the other, and held it down." – Slim during the silence after the dog's death (Chapter 3)
quote_zh: Slim's act of subduing his own hand is a miniature portrait of his character. The verb means to conquer or bring under control, and here Slim applies it to his own body. Steinbeck shows the prince of the ranch, the man with the most authority, physically restraining himself from fidgeting, from speaking, from betraying the emotion of the moment. The gesture acknowledges the gravity of Candy's loss without a single word. In a world where men bark orders and swing fists, Slim's self-subduing is the novel's definition of strength: the power to hold oneself still in the face of pain.
ex_en: Slim subdued his hand as if to demonstrate that true control was not over others but over oneself.
ex_zh: The afternoon heat subdued itself into a weary dusk, the sun pulling a blanket of shadow over its own burning face.

def_en: willing and able to listen to and accept ideas; open to conversation
def_zh: quiet and receptive, receptive audience, receptive to talk
quote_en: "Slim neither encouraged nor discouraged him. He just sat back quiet and receptive." – The narrator describing Slim as George begins to confide (Chapter 3)
quote_zh: Slim's receptivity is the quality that unlocks George's confession. The adjective describes a posture of listening that is both passive and powerful; Slim does not demand George's story, he merely makes space for it. Steinbeck uses Slim as the novel's silent confessor, and 'receptive' captures the rarest quality in the bunkhouse: the willingness to hear another man's truth without judgment. This receptivity stands in stark contrast to Carlson's blunt forcefulness and Curley's pugnacity. It is through Slim's receptive silence that the reader learns about Weed, about Lennie's history, and about the depth of George's reluctant loyalty.
ex_en: Slim's receptive silence was a rare gift in a world where men talked over each other to fill the empty spaces.
ex_zh: The night was receptive to the campfire's tiny glow, holding its light without question, asking for nothing in return.

def_en: a card game played by one person alone
def_zh: play solitaire, solitaire hand, lay out solitaire
quote_en: "George stacked the scattered cards and began to lay out his solitaire hand." – The narrator after George's conversation with Slim (Chapter 3)
quote_zh: George's repeated laying out of solitaire hands is an externalisation of his isolation. Solitaire is a game for one, and its presence underscores the paradox of his life: he is never alone, yet he is perpetually solitary in responsibility. The word derives from the French 'solitaire', meaning alone, and Steinbeck uses the ritual of the solitary card game to show George retreating into a private mental space, a brief escape from the burden of guardianship. The solitaire hand also mirrors the gamble of the dream—a pattern of cards that might or might not come out, dependent on chance as much as planning.
ex_en: Each solitaire hand George laid out was a silent argument with fate, a pattern he could control in a world that offered no such power.
ex_zh: The dream was a game of solitaire played in the heart, where every winning hand depended on a luck that never came.

def_en: the complete absence of sound; a stillness heavy with unspoken meaning
def_zh: silence fell, heavy silence, break the silence
quote_en: "The silence fell on the room again. A minute passed, and another minute." – The narrator after Carlson leads the dog outside (Chapter 3)
quote_zh: Steinbeck personifies 'silence' as an active force that 'fell' and 'invaded' the room, transforming the absence of sound into a palpable presence. This is not a peaceful quiet but a suffocating weight, thick with the unspoken knowledge of what is happening outside. The silence is communal dread given form; every man knows a killing is about to occur, and none will speak to stop it or to comfort Candy. The passage turns silence into a form of complicity. This same silence will return at the novel's end, when George sits stiffly by the river after his own act of mercy, and no words are adequate.
ex_en: The silence that filled the bunkhouse after the shot was louder than any confession, an admission that no one had the courage to intervene.
ex_zh: Silence settled on them like a verdict, the judge having left the bench and the sentence still echoing in the empty air.

def_en: a German semi-automatic pistol; specifically, Carlson's weapon used to kill Candy's dog and later stolen by Lennie
def_zh: got a Luger, carry a Luger, Luger pistol
quote_en: "'The hell I ain't. Got a Luger. It won't hurt him none at all.'" – Carlson brandishing his gun to shoot Candy's dog (Chapter 3)
quote_zh: Carlson's Luger is more than a prop; it is the instrument of mercy and death that will bookend the novel's violence. By naming the specific make, Steinbeck gives the gun a sinister pedigree—a German military pistol, imported into a California bunkhouse, carrying the weight of impersonal efficiency. Carlson's casual confidence that the Luger will cause no pain is the voice of a man who has reduced death to a mechanical problem. The gun will later be stolen by Lennie, the very man who most needs protection, and will finally be turned on him by George in the ultimate act of mercy. The Luger thus becomes the object through which the novel's central question—who has the right to take a life?—is answered.
ex_en: Carlson's Luger was the ranch's dark angel, dispensing a mercy that felt indistinguishable from execution.
ex_zh: The Luger gleamed dully in the lamplight, its barrel a tunnel leading from the bunkhouse into the blackness of final decisions.

def_en: a tool with a broad blade and handle, used for digging; here an instrument of burial
def_zh: take a shovel, get a shovel, shovel and dirt
quote_en: "Slim said, 'Carlson.' 'Yeah?' 'You know what to do.' 'What ya mean, Slim?' 'Take a shovel,' said Slim shortly." – Slim instructing Carlson after the dog's death (Chapter 3)
quote_zh: Slim's command—'take a shovel'—is a stark, two-word acknowledgment that death is not the end of labour. The shovel transforms the sentimental act of pet burial into a practical chore, an extension of the ranch work. Steinbeck uses Slim's brevity to demonstrate that even the kindest man on the ranch accepts the brutal economy of the place: a dead dog must be buried, not mourned. The shovel reappears symbolically at the novel's close, when George must dispose of Lennie's body, and the same grim practicality governs the aftermath. The word insists that death is not a tragedy in this world but a task, one more thing to be done before supper.
ex_en: The shovel that dug the dog's grave was the same one that would have buried any of them, had kindness not taken a different shape.
ex_zh: Grief wore the handle of a shovel smooth that evening, the blade turning the earth like a page in a story no one wanted to read.

def_en: a person who is unable to walk or move properly due to injury or disability
def_zh: old an' a cripple, become a cripple, cripple on the ranch
quote_en: "'I wish somebody'd shoot me if I get old an' a cripple.'" – Slim after endorsing the dog's death (Chapter 3)
quote_zh: Slim's use of 'cripple' is a blunt self-indictment of the ranch's value system. He equates disability with a life not worth living, and the noun carries the brutal logic of a world where only the productive are tolerated. Steinbeck gives this line to Slim, the novel's moral compass, to show that even the wisest character has internalised the ranch's harsh calculus. The word echoes forward to Crooks, who is literally crooked of back, and to Candy, who has lost a hand. In the economy of the ranch, a cripple is a man awaiting a bullet, and Slim's frankness is both a statement of philosophy and a dark prophecy of Candy's fate.
ex_en: The word 'cripple' on Slim's lips was not cruelty but a statement of fact in a place where the broken were discarded.
ex_zh: Time walked with a cripple's limp through the bunkhouse, its uneven gait marking every man's slow approach to uselessness.

def_en: to experience physical or mental pain or distress
def_zh: suffer all the time, suffer in silence, made to suffer
quote_en: "'This ol' dog jus' suffers hisself all the time.'" – Carlson arguing for the dog's death (Chapter 3)
quote_zh: Carlson's phrasing—'suffers hisself'—is grammatically awkward but emotionally precise. The reflexive construction suggests that the dog is both the agent and the victim of its suffering, trapped in a body that has become a prison. Steinbeck uses this colloquial verb to frame suffering as a condition that serves no purpose, a state of mere endurance with no redemption. The argument is the novel's clearest statement of its euthanasia theme: when a creature suffers without hope of improvement, the kindest act is to end it. George will internalise this logic for Lennie, and the word 'suffer' will haunt the final scene by the pool.
ex_en: The dog's daily suffering was a slow rehearsal for the final mercy, a lesson George would later recall with terrible clarity.
ex_zh: Suffering wove itself into the fabric of the bunkhouse, a thread so old and grey that no one remembered the colour it had been before.

def_en: a flowering plant widely grown as feed for livestock; in the novel, a symbol of the self-sustaining farm the men dream of
def_zh: alfalfa patch, feed alfalfa, grow alfalfa
quote_en: "'They's a place for alfalfa and plenty water to flood it.'" – George describing the dream farm (Chapter 3)
quote_zh: Alfalfa is the most concrete detail of the dream farm, and its repeated invocation anchors the fantasy in agricultural reality. The word, with its soft, almost lullabic sound, becomes a verbal talisman for the men, proof that their plan is not idle dreaming but a practical enterprise. Steinbeck uses alfalfa as a synecdoche for the whole dream: it is the food for the rabbits Lennie will tend, the crop that will sustain them, the green evidence that the land is fertile. The word's recurrence throughout the novel makes its final silence—after Lennie's death—a deafening acknowledgment that the dream has withered.
ex_en: Alfalfa was the green thread that stitched the dream together, a promise of growth in a life of dust and transience.
ex_zh: The scent of alfalfa haunted their conversations like the ghost of a harvest that would never be cut.

def_en: female rabbits, especially those kept for breeding
def_zh: the does, a litter of does, breed the does
quote_en: "'Ever' six weeks or so,' George continued, 'them does would throw a litter so we'd have plenty rabbits to eat an' to sell.'" – George expanding the dream (Chapter 3)
quote_zh: The word 'does' is a small, technical term of animal husbandry, and its inclusion in George's litany of the dream signals how carefully he has imagined the practical details of their future. It is not a vague fantasy of rabbits; it is a breeding programme with terminology, cycles, and yields. Steinbeck uses this specificity to make the dream feel achingly real, a plan rather than a pipedream. Yet the biological fecundity of the 'does' also carries an ironic counterpoint: while the rabbits multiply effortlessly, the men's dream proves sterile, incapable of reproducing itself in reality. The does are a fertility that will never be theirs.
ex_en: The does in George's vision multiplied hope itself, each imagined litter a currency of a future that might never arrive.
ex_zh: The word 'does' fell from his lips as softly as a newborn rabbit, blind and pink, seeking a warmth that the world would not provide.

def_en: lacking in quantity or quality; thin, inadequate, or insufficient
def_zh: meager light, meager possessions, meager comfort
quote_en: "In the stable buck's room a small electric globe threw a meager yellow light." – The narrator describing Crooks's room (Chapter 4)
quote_zh: The adjective 'meager' characterises Crooks's entire existence in a single visual detail. The light in his room is not bright or welcoming but thin and barely sufficient, a symbol of the marginal existence forced upon him. Steinbeck uses the word to suggest that even the physical environment conspires to remind Crooks of his place; he is given just enough light to see, just enough space to sleep, but never enough to thrive. This meagerness extends beyond the visual into the emotional: Crooks is starved of companionship, respect, and recognition. The word functions as a quiet indictment of a system that allocates resources—light, dignity, hope—according to race.
ex_en: The meager glow of the single bulb in Crooks's room mirrored the sparse kindness the world had offered him.
ex_zh: Hope in the harness room burned with a meager flame, a candle stub that had learned to survive on almost no wax at all.

def_en: not friendly or forthcoming; cool and distant, often by choice
def_zh: proud and aloof, remain aloof, aloof manner
quote_en: "Crooks was a proud, aloof man. He kept his distance and demanded that other people keep theirs." – The narrator introducing Crooks (Chapter 4)
quote_zh: The word 'aloof' captures the complex self-protection Crooks has constructed. It connotes a deliberate distance, a coldness that is not innate but forged by years of exclusion. By describing Crooks as both 'proud' and 'aloof', Steinbeck insists that his isolation is a response to racism, not a personal failing. Crooks has learned that keeping others away is the only way to preserve his dignity in a world that would strip it. His aloofness is armour, but it is also a prison; the word prefigures the bitter speech he will deliver to Lennie about the sickness of loneliness. The aloof mask cracks only briefly when the dream of the farm offers an alternative, before slamming shut again.
ex_en: Crooks cultivated an aloof exterior, a wall built from the bricks of every insult and rejection he had ever endured.
ex_zh: His aloofness hung around him like a winter coat worn indoors, unnecessary but impossible to take off.

def_en: feeling or expressing contempt or derision for someone or something regarded as worthless
def_zh: scornful laugh, scornful look, scornful tone
quote_en: "Crooks was scornful. 'I seen hundreds of men come by on the road an' on the ranches, with their bindles on their back an' that same damn thing in their heads.'" – Crooks dismissing the dream of land (Chapter 4)
quote_zh: Crooks's scornful dismissal of the dream is an emotional defence disguised as wisdom. The adjective reveals a man whose experience has curdled into cynicism; he has seen too many dreams fail to allow himself to hope. However, his scorn is not pure contempt—it is a preemptive strike against disappointment. Steinbeck uses the word to show how oppression deforms the spirit: Crooks cannot afford to believe, so he must mock. Yet this scorn is fragile; within pages, he will tentatively offer to join the dream, proving that his scornful mask hides a longing just as desperate as Lennie's.
ex_en: Crooks's scornful words about the dream were the armour of a man who had been wounded by hope too many times.
ex_zh: Scorn settled on his tongue like the taste of a bitter herb, familiar and almost comforting in its predictable sting.

def_en: assessed the quality or nature of; judged or evaluated
def_zh: appraised him coolly, appraised the situation, eyes appraised
quote_en: "She appraised him coolly. 'I ain't sure you heard nothing.'" – Curley's wife evaluating Candy's threat to tell (Chapter 4)
quote_zh: The verb 'appraised' reveals Curley's wife as a shrewd reader of power dynamics. In this moment, she is not the flirtatious tart the men label her but a calculating woman weighing risks. She appraises Candy's threat, measures its credibility, and dismisses it. Steinbeck uses this moment of cold assessment to demonstrate that Curley's wife understands the ranch's hierarchy far better than she is given credit for; she knows exactly what threats she can make and which ones can be ignored. The word grants her a momentary agency, an intelligence that makes her later vulnerable conversation with Lennie all the more tragic, for she will fail to appraise the danger he represents.
ex_en: Curley's wife appraised the three men in Crooks's room with the cold eye of someone who had learned to read the slightest shift in power.
ex_zh: The silence appraised him, its invisible scales weighing each word before deciding whether it deserved a reply.

def_en: in a manner showing contempt; scornfully and disrespectfully
def_zh: said contemptuously, laughed contemptuously, dismissed contemptuously
quote_en: "'Awright,' she said contemptuously. 'Awright, cover 'im up if ya wanna. Whatta I care?'" – Curley's wife responding to Candy's lie about Curley's hand (Chapter 4)
quote_zh: Curley's wife's contemptuous reply drips with a bitterness born of being permanently underestimated. She is not fooled by the story of Curley's hand caught in a machine, and her contempt is directed at the men's assumption that she is too stupid to see through their lies. Steinbeck uses the adverb to reveal her intelligence and her resentment in equal measure. Yet her contempt also isolates her; it pushes the men further away, reinforcing the cycle of mutual suspicion. The word captures the tone of a woman who has been given no respect and who has learned to respond with a scorn that mirrors the scorn directed at her.
ex_en: Her contemptuous dismissal of the men's flimsy lie revealed a sharp mind trapped in a role that demanded she play dumb.
ex_zh: The remark dropped contemptuously from her lips like a coin tossed to a beggar, its value measured in the sting of its landing.

def_en: became less intense, violent, or severe; calmed down
def_zh: anger subsided, noise subsided, subsided into silence
quote_en: "Candy subsided. 'No ...' he agreed. 'Nobody'd listen to us.'" – Candy backing down after Curley's wife threatens Crooks (Chapter 4)
quote_zh: The verb 'subsided' captures the deflation of Candy's brief surge of defiance. Earlier, he had stood up and declared that they had their own ranch and were not 'bindle stiffs'. Now, under the pressure of Curley's wife's cruel logic, he collapses. Steinbeck uses 'subsided' to describe more than silence; it is a sinking, a returning to the low position from which he had momentarily risen. The word is a miniature tragedy: for a few moments, the dream had lifted Candy; now, faced with the reality of the ranch's power structure, he subsides back into helplessness, a foreshadowing of the dream's final collapse.
ex_en: Candy subsided under the weight of her threat, his brief defiance crumbling like a sandcastle before the tide.
ex_zh: The hope that had flared so brightly subsided into a grey ash of acceptance, the embers cooling before they had ever truly warmed anyone.

def_en: a pair of glasses worn to correct vision; here symbolising literacy and intellectual separation
def_zh: gold-rimmed spectacles, adjust spectacles, wearing spectacles
quote_en: "And he had books, too; a tattered dictionary and a mauled copy of the California civil code for 1905. There were battered magazines and a few dirty books on a special shelf over his bunk. A pair of large gold-rimmed spectacles hung from a nail on the wall above his bed." – The narrator listing Crooks's possessions (Chapter 4)
quote_zh: Crooks's gold-rimmed spectacles are a potent symbol of his difference from the other men. While the ranch hands spend their leisure time playing cards or visiting whore houses, Crooks reads. The spectacles signify his literacy, his access to books, and his solitary intellectual life. However, they also mark his isolation; the other men do not read, and his glasses set him apart just as his skin colour does. The gold rims suggest a dignity and a care for possessions that the bindle stiffs lack. Steinbeck hangs them on a nail, as if Crooks's intelligence is just another tool to be put aside when not in use, never fully integrated into the life of the ranch.
ex_en: Crooks's gold-rimmed spectacles were windows into a mind that saw further than the bunkhouse walls but was never invited to share the view.
ex_zh: The spectacles caught the meager lamplight and threw it back in two tiny golden accusations, as if the room itself were being examined.

def_en: dialect for 'framing'; falsely incriminating someone; setting them up to take the blame
def_zh: framin' Crooks, framin' someone, accused of framin'
quote_en: "'If you was to do that, we'd tell,' he said quietly. 'We'd tell about you framin' Crooks.'" – Candy threatening Curley's wife after she menaces Crooks (Chapter 4)
quote_zh: The dialect spelling 'framin'' roots the word in the vernacular of the ranch, but the concept it names is sophisticated: the deliberate construction of a false accusation. Curley's wife's power to frame Crooks—to say he assaulted her—is the ultimate expression of her racial privilege. Steinbeck uses the word to expose the terrifying vulnerability of a black man in 1930s America; a single accusation from a white woman could lead to a lynching. The word also underscores Crooks's powerlessness; he cannot even defend himself against a lie. The threat of framing silences Crooks completely, reducing him to the 'nothing' the world has told him he is.
ex_en: The threat of framing Crooks was a weapon Curley's wife wielded without hesitation, knowing that her word would always outweigh his.
ex_zh: The accusation hung in the harness room like a noose, the word 'framin'' tightening around the truth until it could no longer breathe.

def_en: moral or legal entitlements to have or do something
def_zh: have rights, no rights, demand rights
quote_en: "Crooks said, 'Maybe you guys better go. I ain't sure I want you in here no more. A colored man got to have some rights even if he don't like 'em.'" – Crooks ordering Candy and Lennie to leave (Chapter 4)
quote_zh: Crooks's bitter invocation of 'rights' is the philosophical heart of the chapter. He asserts that he has a right to control his own space, yet the word is laced with irony, for he knows he has no rights in any meaningful sense. The statement is a reclaiming of dignity through exclusion—if he cannot join the white men in the bunkhouse, he can at least bar them from his room. Steinbeck uses the word to frame Crooks's tragedy as a political condition: he is a man denied the most basic entitlements of personhood, and his only assertion of power is the right to be alone. The hollow victory of this 'right' is a devastating indictment of the society that has stripped everything else.
ex_en: Crooks's demand for his rights was the cry of a man who had been given nothing but the power to say no.
ex_zh: Rights in the bunkhouse were measured like grain, doled out by the scoop and always falling short of a full sack.

def_en: a liquid or lotion rubbed on the body to relieve pain, especially in muscles and joints
def_zh: bottle of liniment, rub liniment, apply liniment
quote_en: "Crooks sat on his bunk. His shirt was out of his jeans in back. In one hand he held a bottle of liniment, and with the other he rubbed his spine." – The narrator describing Crooks's nightly ritual (Chapter 4)
quote_zh: Crooks's nightly ritual of rubbing liniment into his crooked spine is a portrait of chronic, unglamorous pain.
