The liniment is a medicine for working bodies, yet Crooks applies it in solitude, with no one to share the burden. Steinbeck uses this detail to humanise Crooks immediately after the hostility of his first words to Lennie; the reader sees a man tending his own broken body, a task that requires no witnesses. The liniment becomes a symbol of the ranch's toll on the body—every man has his aches—but also of Crooks's self-reliance, his ability to survive without the help that the other men take for granted.
ex_en: The sharp scent of liniment in Crooks's room was the perfume of a man who had learned to be his own doctor, nurse, and comforter.
ex_zh: Liniment pooled in his palm like the liquid version of a complaint never voiced, its cool burn the only answer to the ache of years.

def_en: bent or twisted out of shape; not straight
def_zh: crooked back, crooked spine, crooked smile
quote_en: "His body was bent over to the left by his crooked spine." – The narrator describing Crooks (Chapter 4)
quote_zh: The adjective 'crooked' physically marks Crooks as different, bent, and damaged. His spine was kicked out of shape by a horse, a violent encounter with the very animals he tends. Steinbeck uses the word to embody Crooks's marginalisation; he is literally 'not straight', a deviation from the normal able body that the ranch demands. Yet this crookedness is also a source of bitter wisdom; Crooks has been bent by life but not broken, and his twisted spine has given him a perspective—from the sidelines—that the other men lack. The word is both a fact of his body and a metaphor for the deformed social order that has crippled him.
ex_en: Crooks's crooked back was the physical embodiment of a world that had bent every black man into a shape that could be kept in the corner.
ex_zh: The fence post stood crooked against the sunset, a wooden spine that had taken one kick too many and learned to live with the lean.

def_en: sad because one has no friends or company; isolated and unhappy
def_zh: awful lonely, get lonely, lonely life
quote_en: "'I tell ya,' he cried, 'I tell ya a guy gets too lonely an' he gets sick.'" – Crooks confiding in Lennie about the effects of isolation (Chapter 4)
quote_zh: The word 'lonely' is the simplest in the novel's vocabulary, yet it carries the weight of its central theme. Curley's wife, Crooks, Candy, and even George are all variations on this single condition. Here, Crooks articulates loneliness not as a passing mood but as a sickness, a pathological state that erodes the self. Steinbeck strips away ornament to let the word land with full force. The plainness of 'lonely' makes it universal; every character is defined by their distance from or proximity to someone else. Crooks's confession transforms the harness room into a surgery where the wound of isolation is laid bare, then quickly covered again.
ex_en: The word 'lonely' was the silent third occupant of every conversation in the bunkhouse, a shadow that never left the room.
ex_zh: Loneliness seeped through the cracks in the barn wall like the evening chill, finding every living thing and settling in its bones.

def_en: old and torn; in poor condition from much use
def_zh: tattered dictionary, tattered clothes, tattered copy
quote_en: "And he had books, too; a tattered dictionary and a mauled copy of the California civil code for 1905." – The narrator listing Crooks's possessions (Chapter 4)
quote_zh: The adjective 'tattered' applied to Crooks's dictionary and law book suggests a man who has used his reading material to the point of disintegration. These are not decorative books; they are worn tools, evidence of a mind constantly at work. The law book is particularly poignant; a black man in 1930s America reading the civil code is a man searching for rights in a document that has denied them to him. The tattered pages suggest years of rereading, of looking for a loophole in the law or in language itself, a futile but dignified labour of the mind. Steinbeck uses the condition of the books to speak for Crooks's inner life, which is rich and tattered in equal measure.
ex_en: The tattered pages of Crooks's books were the battlefields on which he had fought his long, private war against ignorance and exclusion.
ex_zh: His dreams lay tattered in the corner like the old dictionary, every page turned so many times that the words had begun to blur into a single, unreadable plea.

def_en: a cruel slang term for a stupid or foolish person; an insult
def_zh: call someone a dum‑dum, a dum‑dum and a lousy ol' sheep, treat like a dum‑dum
quote_en: "'Standin' here talkin' to a bunch of bindle stiffs—a nigger an' a dum‑dum and a lousy ol' sheep—an' likin' it because they ain't nobody else.'" – Curley's wife lashing out at the men in Crooks's room (Chapter 4)
quote_zh: Curley's wife's use of 'dum‑dum' to describe Lennie is a moment of casual cruelty that exposes her own pain. The insult reduces Lennie to his mental disability, a target she knows will wound because she, too, has been wounded. Steinbeck uses the word to show how the hierarchy of victimisation works: those who are oppressed—Curley's wife by her husband, the men by their poverty—find someone lower still to despise. Yet the cruelty of the word also reveals her desperation; she is lashing out because her attempt at connection has been rejected. The 'dum‑dum' is the person she can hurt because everyone else has hurt her.
ex_en: The word 'dum‑dum' dropped from Curley's wife's lips like a stone thrown by someone drowning, an act of cruelty born of her own desperate buoyancy.
ex_zh: The insult hung in the harness room like a cracked bell, its harsh ring fading into the silence of three men who had been called worse by better people.

def_en: having talons or claw-like prongs; resembling the claws of a bird of prey
def_zh: four-taloned fork, taloned grip, taloned hand
quote_en: "One end of the great barn was piled high with new hay and over the pile hung the four-taloned jackson fork suspended from its pulley." – The narrator describing the barn setting before the tragedy (Chapter 5)
quote_zh: The 'four-taloned jackson fork' hanging over the hay transforms a piece of farm equipment into a predatory presence. Steinbeck could have described the fork simply as 'four-pronged', but 'taloned' evokes the curved claws of a hawk or eagle, injecting the quiet barn with a latent menace. The fork dangles from its pulley like a bird of prey waiting to strike, a silent reminder that the barn is not only a place of refuge but also of potential violence. This image of suspended danger foreshadows the death that will occur beneath it, as if the barn itself were waiting for the kill.
ex_en: The taloned fork above the hay pile was a silent omen, its iron claws poised like fate over the quiet Sunday afternoon.
ex_zh: The hay dust swirled in the sunbeams while the taloned fork hung motionless above, a metallic spider dreaming of the moment it would drop.

def_en: thin, delicate strands or pieces of something; small scattered amounts
def_zh: wisps of hay, wisps of hair, wisps of cloud
quote_en: "The resting horses nibbled the remaining wisps of hay, and they stamped their feet and they bit the wood of the mangers and rattled the halter chains." – The narrator describing the quiet barn (Chapter 5)
quote_zh: The word 'wisps' suggests scarcity and fragility. The horses are nibbling the very last fragments of hay, an image of quiet depletion that mirrors the emotional state of the men on the ranch. Each character holds onto some wisp of hope, dignity, or dream, as thin and insubstantial as the scattered strands the horses mouth. Steinbeck uses this small, precise detail to establish an atmosphere of dwindling—time is running out, resources are thin, and the promise of abundance is as scattered as the last wisps of feed. The word prepares the reader for a scene in which everything will unravel.
ex_en: The horses pulling at the last wisps of hay were a picture of the men themselves, each one grasping at the thin remnants of a dream.
ex_zh: Her words fell like wisps of straw from a broken bale, each one too light to build anything with, yet too sharp to ignore.

def_en: twisted and turned the body, especially in pain, struggle, or discomfort
def_zh: writhed to be free, body writhed, writhed in agony
quote_en: "She struggled violently under his hands. Her feet battered on the hay and she writhed to be free; and from under Lennie's hand came a muffled screaming." – The narrator describing Curley's wife's death struggle (Chapter 5)
quote_zh: Steinbeck's choice of 'writhed' emphasizes the physical desperation of Curley's wife's final moments. The verb connotes a serpentine twisting, a body contorting against an inescapable grip, and it strips the scene of any romanticism. Unlike a simple 'struggled', 'writhed' suggests a loss of bodily control, a primal, animal reflex. The word aligns with the novel's broader imagery of animals caught in traps; Curley's wife, who earlier was compared to a rattrap, now writhes like the creature caught inside it. The violence of the verb makes the scene almost unbearable, forcing the reader to witness the full horror of what Lennie's innocent strength can do.
ex_en: As she writhed under Lennie's uncomprehending hands, the barn became a trap from which neither of them could escape.
ex_zh: The river writhed in its bed like a feverish patient, twisting from bank to bank as if trying to shake off the weight of the evening.

def_en: a restless dissatisfaction; a longing for something different or better
def_zh: aching discontent, murmur of discontent, discontent in her face
quote_en: "And the meanness and the plannings and the discontent and the ache for attention were all gone from her face." – The narrator describing Curley's wife after her death (Chapter 5)
quote_zh: 'Discontent' is the third in a sequence of four nouns—meanness, plannings, discontent, ache—that collectively define Curley's wife's life. The word captures a restless dissatisfaction that was never named while she lived; she was always looking, wanting, searching for something she could not articulate. Steinbeck places it in a list that resolves into death, where all four vanish, and her face becomes 'sweet and young'. The implication is devastating: only in death can a woman's discontent be silenced, her restlessness finally stilled. The word becomes an indictment of a world that offered her no outlet for her dissatisfaction except performance and provocation.
ex_en: The discontent that had defined Curley's wife's short life was finally erased, leaving only the peaceful face of a girl who had once dreamed of the movies.
ex_zh: Discontent rustled through the bunkhouse like a mouse in the walls, heard but never caught, gnawing at the edges of every man's sleep.

def_en: schemes, intentions, or designs for the future; often restless or futile
def_zh: plannings and schemes, restless plannings, plannings gone wrong
quote_en: "And the meanness and the plannings and the discontent and the ache for attention were all gone from her face." – The narrator describing Curley's wife after death (Chapter 5)
quote_zh: The plural 'plannings'—a non-standard form—conveys a sense of constant, fidgeting scheming that never solidified into action. Curley's wife spent her life planning escape routes that never opened: the show, the movies, a way out of her marriage. Steinbeck uses the word to suggest that her ambitions were multiple, fragmented, and ultimately futile, a series of plans that circled without landing. The word's grammatical oddness mirrors her own unsettled existence. In death, the plannings stop, and the word's absence from her face is as telling as its presence had been: she is finally at rest from the exhausting labour of hoping.
ex_en: The plannings that had animated Curley's wife were extinguished in an instant, leaving a stillness that was more honest than any dream she had chased.
ex_zh: His plannings scattered like startled birds at the first gunshot of reality, the sky emptying of every future he had imagined.

def_en: unkindness, cruelty, or small-minded nastiness
def_zh: casual meanness, meanness of spirit, meanness and pettiness
quote_en: "And the meanness and the plannings and the discontent and the ache for attention were all gone from her face." – The narrator describing Curley's wife after death (Chapter 5)
quote_zh: The word 'meanness' is startling in its bluntness, and its placement as the first in the list of four nouns forces the reader to acknowledge that Curley's wife could be cruel. Steinbeck does not sanitise her; she was capable of cruelty, as seen in her treatment of Crooks. Yet the word also implicitly asks where that meanness came from—a loveless marriage, constant suspicion, the casual cruelty of the men who called her a tart. The meanness that vanishes from her dead face was a learned response to a world that offered her nothing but contempt. The word is an indictment not only of her character but of the conditions that shaped it.
ex_en: The meanness that hardened Curley's wife's features in life was a mask worn so long it had nearly become her face.
ex_zh: Meanness lived in the bunkhouse like a draft, seeping under the door from the colder rooms of loneliness and fear.

def_en: a continuous dull pain; a persistent longing or yearning
def_zh: ache for attention, deep ache, dull ache
quote_en: "And the meanness and the plannings and the discontent and the ache for attention were all gone from her face." – The narrator describing Curley's wife after death (Chapter 5)
quote_zh: The noun 'ache' transforms a medical word into an emotional diagnosis. Curley's wife did not simply want attention; she ached for it, the desire registering as a physical pain that never subsided. Steinbeck uses the word to dignify her desperation; an ache is not a whim or a vanity but a profound, unrelenting need. The ache was the engine of her behaviour—the flirting, the wandering into bunkhouses, the confiding in strangers. By naming it, Steinbeck asks the reader to see her not as a tart but as a starving soul. The ache vanishes only in death, suggesting that the only cure for such loneliness was oblivion.
ex_en: The ache for someone to truly see her was the wound Curley's wife carried into every room, and it bled onto everyone she touched.
ex_zh: An ache for home settled in his chest, a dull and faithful companion that walked every mile beside him.

def_en: rough and harsh-sounding, especially of a voice strained by screaming or emotion
def_zh: hoarse cry, hoarse whisper, voice hoarse
quote_en: "He moved his hand a little and her hoarse cry came out." – The narrator describing the muffled sound of Curley's wife's scream (Chapter 5)
quote_zh: The adjective 'hoarse' captures the physical degradation of Curley's wife's final scream. The cry that escapes Lennie's hand is not a clear, piercing shriek but a roughened, damaged sound, as if her voice has already been worn down by the struggle and fear. Steinbeck uses the word to convey the brutal bodily reality of the killing; her throat is raw, her breath strained, her voice reduced to a ragged remnant. The hoarseness also suggests that she has been screaming for attention in one way or another her entire married life, and now, in the moment of greatest need, her voice is still rough and insufficient.
ex_en: The hoarse cry that broke from under Lennie's hand was the last sound of a woman whose voice had never been truly heard.
ex_zh: The wind howled a hoarse complaint through the cracks of the barn, as if the building itself were trying to warn someone who could not listen.

def_en: a sudden, uncontrollable fear that causes wild, irrational behaviour
def_zh: in a panic, seized by panic, rising panic
quote_en: "Lennie was in a panic. His face was contorted. She screamed then, and Lennie's other hand closed over her mouth and nose." – The narrator describing Lennie's reaction (Chapter 5)
quote_zh: The word 'panic' identifies the precise emotional mechanism that destroys Curley's wife. Lennie does not act out of anger, lust, or malice but out of a terrified, reflexive need to silence her. Steinbeck uses 'panic' to remind the reader that Lennie is as trapped as his victim; his mind, unable to process the escalating situation, reverts to the simplest possible solution: cover the noise. The word connects this moment back to Weed, where another scream triggered another panicked grip. The tragedy is that panic, not evil, drives the fatal action, making Lennie both killer and victim in a single, involuntary spasm.
ex_en: Lennie's panic in the barn was the same blind terror that had driven him into the irrigation ditch at Weed, a pattern that had been waiting to repeat itself.
ex_zh: Panic rose in his throat like floodwater, cold and fast, swallowing every thought before it could reach his lips.

def_en: comforted someone in a time of grief, loss, or disappointment
def_zh: consoled him gently, tried to console, consoled with words
quote_en: "She consoled him. 'Don't you worry none. He was jus' a mutt. You can get another one easy.'" – Curley's wife comforting Lennie over the dead puppy (Chapter 5)
quote_zh: The verb 'consoled' reveals Curley's wife's capacity for kindness, a side of her character the men never see. Lennie is distraught over the dead puppy, and she responds with genuine, if clumsy, comfort. The moment is achingly human: two profoundly lonely people connect over a small death. Steinbeck uses this brief act of consolation to heighten the tragedy; the woman who consoles Lennie now will be dead by his hand in minutes. The word suggests what might have been—a friendship, an understanding—if the world of the ranch had not so thoroughly poisoned every possibility of innocent connection.
ex_en: Curley's wife consoled Lennie with a gentleness that surprised even herself, a reminder that loneliness sometimes bred unlikely tenderness.
ex_zh: The rain consoled the dry earth with a thousand tiny touches, each drop a whispered promise that life would return.

def_en: a mongrel dog of mixed breed; here dismissively applied to the dead puppy
def_zh: just a mutt, a stray mutt, worthless mutt
quote_en: "'Don't you worry none. He was jus' a mutt. You can get another one easy.'" – Curley's wife trying to reassure Lennie about the dead puppy (Chapter 5)
quote_zh: When Curley's wife dismisses the dead puppy as 'jus' a mutt', she echoes the ranch's brutal logic of disposability. The word reduces the puppy to its lack of pedigree, its replaceability, just as the men are replaceable and the old dog was replaceable. Yet her dismissiveness is also a form of consolation; she is telling Lennie that the loss is not catastrophic. The irony is devastating: the man who has just killed a puppy out of misplaced affection will in moments kill her, and she will be mourned even less than the mutt. Steinbeck uses the word to underline the novel's harshest truth—some lives are deemed worthless before they are even over.
ex_en: The word 'mutt' on Curley's wife's lips was both a dismissal of the dead puppy and an unconscious prophecy of her own disposable fate.
ex_zh: He loved her like a mutt loves its owner, without pedigree or reason, simply because she had once bent down and noticed him.

def_en: pleasant to touch; not hard or firm; yielding gently to pressure
def_zh: soft things, soft hair, soft and fine
quote_en: "Curley's wife moved away from him a little. 'I think you're nuts,' she said. 'No I ain't,' Lennie explained earnestly. 'George says I ain't. I like to pet nice things with my fingers, sof' things.'" – Lennie to Curley's wife, revealing his fatal compulsion (Chapter 5)
quote_zh: The word 'soft' is the key to Lennie's tragedy. It is not complexity, ambition, or desire that undoes him but an almost infantile love of tactile gentleness. Throughout the novel, 'soft' things—mice, puppies, velvet, hair—draw Lennie with a compulsion he cannot control or understand. Steinbeck uses this simple adjective to illustrate the heartbreaking paradox of Lennie's character: the gentlest instinct leads to the most violent outcomes. In this exchange, Lennie defends his nature against Curley's wife's mockery, and moments later that same love of softness will close his hands around her hair and then her neck. The word recurs like a refrain, each repetition building the inevitability of the final, fatal encounter with something soft that will not survive his touch.
ex_en: Lennie's fatal love of soft things was the novel's cruelest irony: the tenderness in his hands was precisely what made them lethal.
ex_zh: Her voice was soft as the underbelly of a rabbit, and just as fragile when the weight of the world pressed down upon it.

def_en: in a sly, cunning way; using cleverness to achieve a goal, often by indirect means
def_zh: speak craftily, craftily manipulate, craftily persuade
quote_en: "Lennie said craftily—'Tell me like you done before.' 'Tell you what?' 'About the rabbits.'" – Lennie coaxing George into the familiar ritual of the dream (Chapter 6)
quote_zh: The adverb 'craftily' transforms Lennie from a helpless dependent into a skilled manipulator, if only for a moment. Throughout the novel, Lennie has been defined by his simplicity, yet here he employs a deliberate strategy—using George's own ritual of storytelling against him—to extract comfort. Steinbeck's choice reveals that Lennie understands George's emotional needs better than anyone; he knows the dream is a lullaby that soothes them both. The word 'craftily' is laced with tragic irony, for Lennie's cunning is deployed not to escape punishment but to hear once more the story of a future that is already irrevocably lost. This brief flash of intelligence only deepens the pathos of what is about to happen.
ex_en: Lennie's craftily phrased request showed that even the simplest mind could learn to steer the heart of the one who loved him.
ex_zh: Hope crept into his voice craftily, like a fox slipping through a gap in the fence that sorrow had forgotten to mend.

def_en: in a hostile, aggressive, and ready-to-fight manner
def_zh: retorted belligerently, shouted belligerently, belligerently defensive
quote_en: "But the rabbit repeated softly over and over, 'He gonna leave you, ya crazy bastard...' Now Lennie retorted belligerently, 'He ain't neither. George won't do nothing like that.'" – Lennie arguing with the hallucinated rabbit (Chapter 6)
quote_zh: The adverb 'belligerently' appears at a moment of pure hallucination, as Lennie defends George against an accusation made by his own mind. The word suggests combat readiness, a pugnacity that Lennie has never shown toward any actual person. Steinbeck uses this internal belligerence to illustrate the fracture in Lennie's psyche; he is fighting phantoms now, his defence of George taking on a desperate, aggressive edge. The rabbit, a projection of Lennie's guilt and fear, pushes him to this rare assertion, yet the belligerence is futile—directed at a figment, it changes nothing. The word marks the final stage of Lennie's mental unravelling before George arrives to end it.
ex_en: Even in his hallucinations, Lennie's belligerent defence of George proved that his loyalty was the fiercest part of him.
ex_zh: The storm clouds gathered belligerently on the horizon, their dark fists clenching and unclenching as if daring the valley to stand its ground.

def_en: ran hurriedly with short, quick steps; often used of small animals fleeing
def_zh: scuttled away, scuttled back, scuttled into hiding
quote_en: "George came quietly out of the brush and the rabbit scuttled back into Lennie's brain." – The narrator as George appears at the pool (Chapter 6)
quote_zh: The verb 'scuttled' merges the animal and the psychological into a single, eerie image. The rabbit, a hallucination that has been tormenting Lennie, flees like a real creature, its movement described with a word typically reserved for crabs, mice, and insects. Steinbeck uses 'scuttled' to blur the boundary between Lennie's inner torment and the external world; the rabbit disappears not by fading but by physically retreating into the brain that produced it. This suggests that Lennie's fears are not dispelled by George's arrival—only forced back underground. The word also echoes earlier descriptions of lizards 'skittering' in Chapter 1, bookending the novel with images of small, frightened creatures fleeing a larger presence.
ex_en: The fantasy of the farm scuttled back into the darkness of Lennie's mind the moment George appeared, like a mouse retreating from a sudden light.
ex_zh: The last shred of hope scuttled across the floor of his heart, a tiny, panicked thing searching for a corner that grief had not yet claimed.

def_en: in a trembling, unsteady manner, especially from fear, emotion, or weakness
def_zh: said shakily, reached out shakily, shakily stood
quote_en: "George took off his hat. He said shakily, 'Take off your hat, Lennie. The air feels fine.'" – George preparing to shoot Lennie (Chapter 6)
quote_zh: George's voice comes out 'shakily', and this single adverb conveys more than pages of description could. The man who has been the steady one, the voice of command and control, is now trembling. Steinbeck uses this physical tremor to reveal the emotional cost of what George is about to do; his hand and voice shake because he is about to kill the person he loves most. The adverb also humanises George in his darkest moment—he is not a cold executioner but a friend whose entire body revolts against the act. The shakiness is the body's protest against a mercy the mind has resolved to perform.
ex_en: George's shakily spoken instruction to Lennie was the fracture in his composure that revealed the full depth of his love.
ex_zh: The candle flame burned shakily in the draught, as if the light itself were trembling at the edge of a goodbye it could not bear to speak.

def_en: received a sudden, sharp shock or jolt; shook violently from an impact
def_zh: jarred forward, jarred by the shot, body jarred
quote_en: "The crash of the shot rolled up the hills and rolled down again. Lennie jarred, and then settled slowly forward to the sand, and he lay without quivering." – The narrator describing Lennie's death (Chapter 6)
quote_zh: The verb 'jarred' captures the single, violent instant of death with devastating economy. Lennie does not scream, thrash, or cry out; his body simply jolts, a mechanical reaction to the bullet. Steinbeck uses 'jarred' to strip the moment of melodrama, rendering it stark and almost clinical. The word suggests a sudden, external force disrupting a calm state, much like Lennie's own life was a series of jarring disruptions to the world around him. After the jar, Lennie 'settled', a verb that connotes a peaceful coming to rest. The transition from jarred to settled is the novel's final act of gentleness: violence and stillness, sound and silence, all compressed into two verbs.
ex_en: Lennie jarred once with the shot and was still, a gentle giant toppled into a peace he had never found in life.
ex_zh: The rifle's report jarred the silence like a stone thrown into a mirror, the ripples of sound shattering the reflection of what had been.

def_en: trembling or shaking with a slight, rapid motion
def_zh: lay without quivering, quivering lip, quivering with fear
quote_en: "The crash of the shot rolled up the hills and rolled down again. Lennie jarred, and then settled slowly forward to the sand, and he lay without quivering." – The narrator describing Lennie's death (Chapter 6)
quote_zh: The phrase 'without quivering' is the final descriptive detail of Lennie's body, and its negation carries profound weight. Throughout the novel, Lennie quivered with fear, with excitement, with the effort of understanding a world too complex for him. His giant frame was always in motion—dragging feet, flapping hands, trembling lip. That all motion ceases now, that the great body lies still 'without quivering', is the novel's most devastating statement of finality. Steinbeck uses the absence of quivering to mark the transition from life to death, from agitation to peace. Lennie, who could never be still in life, achieves perfect stillness only in death.
ex_en: The absence of quivering in Lennie's body was the first true rest he had ever known, a stillness that George had given him out of love.
ex_zh: The leaves hung without quivering in the breathless air, as if the whole world had paused to witness the cost of mercy.

def_en: in a rigid, inflexible manner, as if the body is locked with tension or shock
def_zh: sat stiffly, moved stiffly, stood stiffly
quote_en: "But George sat stiffly on the bank and looked at his right hand that had thrown the gun away." – The narrator after George shoots Lennie (Chapter 6)
quote_zh: George's posture—sitting 'stiffly'—externalises the emotional paralysis that follows his act. The adverb suggests a body locked in shock, a man who has just committed the most intimate violence imaginable and can no longer move freely. Steinbeck uses this physical rigidity to convey a grief too immense for expression; George is not weeping or wailing but frozen, his body a monument to the decision he had to make. The detail of his gaze fixed on his right hand—the hand that pulled the trigger—adds to the stiffness a dimension of dissociation, as if the hand belongs to someone else. Stiffness is the body's form of silence, and George will carry it out of the clearing.
ex_en: George sat stiffly by the river, his body as rigid as the moral line he had walked between mercy and murder.
ex_zh: The morning after the funeral, she rose stiffly from the chair, her joints remembering the weight of a grief that sleep could not loosen.

def_en: in a weary, exhausted manner, showing the effects of prolonged strain
def_zh: said tiredly, sighed tiredly, spoke tiredly
quote_en: "Carlson was standing over George. 'How'd you do it?' he asked. 'I just done it,' George said tiredly." – George responding to Carlson's question about how he killed Lennie (Chapter 6)
quote_zh: George's monosyllabic answer delivered 'tiredly' is the emotional exhaustion of a man who has spent every reserve he possessed. The adverb 'tiredly' strips his voice of all inflection, all defence, all willingness to explain. He does not justify, excuse, or elaborate; he simply states a fact with the flattened tone of someone who has nothing left. Steinbeck uses this profound weariness to show that George has been drained by the very act of caring for Lennie—the constant vigilance, the protection, the love—and now, at the end, he is empty. The tiredness is not just physical but existential, the crash after years of a burden that has finally been set down.
ex_en: George's tiredly spoken confession was the sound of a soul that had used its last strength to do the hardest thing it would ever do.
ex_zh: Time moved tiredly through the aftermath, each second dragging its feet like a mourner who had walked too many funerals to remember which grief was which.

def_en: dull, flat, and without variation in pitch or tone; lacking expressiveness
def_zh: monotonous voice, monotonous drone, monotonous rhythm
quote_en: "He said woodenly, 'If I was alone I could live so easy.' His voice was monotonous, had no emphasis." – The narrator as George begins the final telling of the dream (Chapter 6)
quote_zh: George's voice, once animated by the telling of the dream, now sounds 'monotonous'. The adjective signals that the story has lost its magic; it is being recited by rote, not with hope but with the grim purpose of a ritual that must be completed. Steinbeck uses this flatness to show that George already knows the dream is over, that he is telling it not as a vision of the future but as a last gift to Lennie, a familiar song to ease him into death. The monotone is the sound of love stripped of illusion; George no longer believes in the little farm, but he knows Lennie needs to hear about it one final time. The absence of emphasis is itself the emphasis of grief.
ex_en: George's monotonous retelling of the dream was a lullaby sung by a man who had lost his faith but not his kindness.
ex_zh: The rain fell with a monotonous persistence, each drop the same as the last, until the world outside the window blurred into a grey hum of acceptance.

def_en: a natural hollow space in the earth or hillside, large enough for a person to enter; a place of primitive shelter
def_zh: find a cave, live in a cave, hide in a cave
quote_en: "'Well, I can go away,' said Lennie. 'I'll go right off in the hills an' find a cave if you don' want me.'" – Lennie offering to leave George (Chapter 6)
quote_zh: Lennie's offer to find a cave and live alone is the novel's most poignant image of self-imposed exile. The cave represents a regression to a pre-social state, a life stripped of companionship, ketchup, rabbits—everything that makes existence bearable. Steinbeck uses the cave as a symbol of the absolute loneliness that awaits anyone cast out from human connection. It is Lennie's version of Crooks's harness room, Candy's bunkhouse corner, Curley's wife's twoby‑four house: a space of isolation entered not by choice but by rejection. The word also echoes the 'jungle‑up' of the novel's opening, completing the circle from transient camp to permanent, solitary shelter. That George refuses to let Lennie go to the cave—and instead leads him to death—is the novel's terrible declaration that some forms of belonging are worth dying for.
ex_en: The cave Lennie imagined as a refuge was the hollow echo of every lonely life on the ranch, a darkness waiting to swallow those who had nobody.
ex_zh: Regret hollowed out a cave in his chest, its walls smooth from the years of words he had never said, its darkness complete and patient.
